Notice Of Motion To Tax Costs
Off Calendar Feb-17-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Jeff Pollack (510-464-8094; jeff@jplawpractice.com) for Plaintiff.
Craig Crawford (ccrawford@carr-mcclellan.com) of Carr nMcClellan P.C. for Defendants.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Bing Fu Kung's motion to tax costs is granted in part, as follows.

Item No. 1 Filing and Motion Fees ($1,136.49). Reduced by $474.43 to $892.95 based on Defendants' stipulation.

Item No. 5 Service of Process ($4,220.04). Reduced by $595 to $3,625.04 to remove a duplicate entry based on Defendants' stipulation. Denied as to the remaining entries. As the prevailing parties in this action, Defendants are entitled as a matter of right to recover all of their costs in this action, regardless of the particular cross-action or pleading to which the costs may relate. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1032(a)(4),(b).)

Item No. 15 Fees for Hosting Electronic Documents ($19,628.04). Motion to tax is granted. Fees for the hosting of electronic documents are allowable as costs only "if a court requires or orders a party to have documents hosted by an electronic filing service provider." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1033.5(a)(14).) Only items not mentioned in section 1032 (i.e., as allowable under subdivision (a) or not allowable under subdivision (b)) may be allowed or denied in the court's discretion. (Sec. 1033.5(c)(4); Ripley v. Pappadopoulos (1994) 23 Cal.App.4th 1616, 1623 ["Subdivision (c)(4) provides that items not otherwise listed in section 1033.5 and items assessed upon application may be allowed or denied in the court's discretion." (emphasis added)].) Thus, neither fees for hosting electronic documents nor the costs of litigation technical support is allowable. (Science Applications Internat. Corp. v. Superior Court (1995) 39 Cal.App.4th 1095, 1103 [holding that expense for document control and database for internal case management was not allowable].

Item No. 16 Other Fees ($12,258.24). Reduced by $100 based on Defendants' stipulation. Motion to tax is granted in the amount of $11,380.50. The costs of computerized legal research are not recoverable under Ladas v. California State Auto. Assoc. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 776. While Defendants criticize Ladas as "legally dubious," it is binding on this Court. (Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455.)

Thus, Plaintiffs are entitled to recover costs in the amount of $14,316.54 [$46,494.51 minus the following amounts: $474.43, $595, $19,628.04, $100, and $11,380.50].)

Revised Notice Of Hearing Regarding Plaintiffs Motion For New Trial Concerning The Courts Order Granting Defendants Motion For Summary Judgment Entered October 27, 2020
Off Calendar Dec-09-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference
Jeff Pollack ; Tele: (510) 464-8094, Plaintiff Bing Fu
Craig Crawford 650-342-9600; Carr Mcclellan PC, Defendant christopher Carthy, Armentum Partners, LLC

ARGUED.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Plaintiff's motion for new trial is denied.

The Court recognizes that its summary judgment ruling erroneously concluded that the 3-year statute of limitations for fraud under Code of Civil Procedure section 338 applied to the rescission claim. Plaintiff's motion for new trial correctly notes (for the first time) that a four-year statute of limitations applies to a claim for rescission based on fraud. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 337(c).)

Notwithstanding that error, the Court determines that defendants are still entitled to summary judgment. Plaintiff released his claims against defendants. Plaintiff released Armentum and Carthy from among other things "any and all past or future claims, demands, causes of action of any kind ... " (Section 3) and all unknown claims (Section 4). (Complaint, Ex. B.) At plaintiff's additional material facts 60-62, plaintiff declares that Carthy, as a fiduciary, never disclosed that plaintiff was releasing payments that Gatekeeper would make to Armentum in the future. "The scope of a fiduciary's obligations depends on the specific facts of the case. [Citation.] Such factors may include, for example, the relative sophistication and experience of the vulnerable party." (Desert Outdoor Advertising v. Superior Court (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 866, 873 [lawyer did not have a fiduciary duty to explain an arbitration clause in a fee agreement to clients who were sophisticated businesspersons]; see also Mt. Holyoke Homes, L.P. v. Jeffer Mangels Butler & Mitchell, LLP (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 1299, 1308-1309 [lawyer had no obligation to explain arbitration agreement to sophisticated client].) Similarly, in this case, the release was clear and explicit and plaintiff does not adduce any facts showing that he was unsophisticated, vulnerable, or could not read or understand the release. The only reasonable inference from the record is that Carthy was acting in good faith at the time of the release and rebuts any presumption of concealment. Carthy did not conceal the plain meaning of the release, which appears to be the basis of plaintiff's additional facts 60-62. Carthy also did not hide whether Gateway would ever profit from the DFCI molecule. The parties entered into the release on September 8, 2010 (Additional Material Fact, 60) and the Novartis settlement regarding the molecule, which compensated Carthy, occurred on September 24, 2013.

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-23-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jeff Pollack, esq. (510-965-3439) for Plaintiff via courtcall.
Thomas J. Gray., esq. (650-342-9600) of Carr McClellan P.C. for Defendant via courtcall.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT BING KUNG's DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT Christopher Carthy's cross-complaint is sustained without leave to amend. Cross-defendant is correct that the Termination and Release Agreement does not contain a contractual provision permitting the recovery of attorneys' fees. (Cross-Compl., Ex. A.) "Except as attorney's fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties?" (Code Civ. Proc. 1021.) Without an attorney's fees clause in the Release, the parties are not entitled to recover attorneys' fees and costs as damages. (Navellier v. Sletten (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 763, 777 ["[S]uch fees and costs are not recoverable in an action for breach of a release unless the agreement or a statute specifically provides for them."].) Cross-complainant has not provided any authority supporting his argument that absent a contractual provision he is still permitted to recover attorneys' fees and costs as damages. Cross-complainant relies on Monster, LLC v. Superior Court (2017) 12 Cal.App.5th 1214; in that case, however the contract did contain a contractual provision expressly authorizing recovery of attorneys' fees and costs by the prevailing party in any action to enforce its rights under the agreement. (Id. at 1220.)

Order is signed.

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO To File A Cross-Complaint And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Dec-24-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Thomas Gray, Esq. of CARR MCCLELLAN, T:650.342.9600, for defendant/moving party;
Jeff Pollack, Esq. T:510.464.8094, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT CHRISTOPHER CARTHY's MOTION FOR LEAVE TO To File A Cross-Complaint, as follows:

Defendant Christopher Carthy's motion to file cross-complaint is granted. Granting the motion comports with California's liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings. (See, e.g., Howard v. County of San Diego (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1422, 1428 ["The policy favoring amendment is so strong that it is a rare case in which denial of leave to amend can be justified."].) If Plaintiff Bing Fu Kung or newly-named cross-defendant John Chant wishes to challenge the proposed claims, they may do so by demurring to the proposed cross-complaint. (See Brown v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 824, 843 ["The 'better course of action' is to allow a plaintiff to amend the complaint 'and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings.'"].) Given that the trial date has been continued to May 18, 2020, Plaintiff has not shown it will be prejudiced if Defendant is granted leave to file the cross-complaint.

Order signed.
